summary: Khailo announces Firestr v0.1, a peer-to-peer platform where users send running software to each other instead of text messages. Built on a decentralized, push-based model using Lua, it aims to make programming so easy that children can create and share networked applications without understanding server infrastructure.
key_points: Firestr is a P2P platform where software is pushed to peers and runs immediately, rather than being downloaded from servers
Apps are written in Lua with a built-in collaborative code editor, eliminating the complexity of web development stacks
The platform is designed to be simple enough for children to start programming and sharing creations
Communication is decentralized and trust-based, with planned encryption for all connections
The UI deliberately mimics a chat application to make interactivity and time flow feel natural
money_quotes: Imagine a P2P chat program where instead of sending messages of text, sound, or video, you send software, that runs immediately and automatically connected.
A child understands the idea of passing a note to another, and this is how Firestr works.
It is a push model, not a pull model. Software is not downloaded from someone, but uploaded to someone.
Even open source and free software web applications require understanding how to setup http servers, databases, etc. You cannot simply give someone software for them to copy and modify.
key_insight: By reimagining software distribution as peer-to-peer message passing rather than client-server downloading, programming and sharing networked applications can become as intuitive as passing notes between friends.
